aliases: Ayam Penyet with tempeh and tofu

english: Ayam Penyet with tempeh and tofu

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term exists for Ayam Penyet. FOODON_03302051 (fried chicken food product) is used as the closest parent. The smashed preparation and sambal accompaniments are not captured by this code.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LN (Chicken meat, fried) is the closest defensible parent. Ayam Penyet includes smashing, marination with spices, and accompaniments (sambal, tempeh, tofu, salad) not captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for Ayam Penyet as a composite dish. SNOMED CT does not have a specific concept for this Indonesian preparation.

main_ingredients: spice marinade (galangal, turmeric, lemongrass, garlic, shallots)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Ayam Penyet is approximately 400 g, comprising one deep-fried smashed chicken thigh (approximately 200 g), sambal, tempeh, tofu, fried batter, and a small side salad.

nutrient_source.title: Fried chicken thigh, battered and fried; with reference to Indonesian composite dish components (tempeh, tofu, sambal) from FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table (2020)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved source entry exists for Ayam Penyet as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for fried chicken thigh (NDB 05165, Chicken, thigh, meat and skin, cooked, fried, battered) weighted at approximately 50% of serving, combined with FAO/INFOODS SEA values for tempeh, tofu, and sambal components. Per-100g values represent a weighted composite of the full plated dish. Energy and macros are cross-checked against HPB Singapore Foodo category data for similar fried chicken dishes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed fried chicken and legume-based dishes; analogous estimate based on fried chicken (GI ~46) and tempeh/tofu components (GI ~25–35) from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Ayam Penyet as a composite dish. GI estimated at 48 (low category) based on weighted analogy: fried battered chicken thigh GI ~46 (University of Sydney GI Database, food ID 1328), tempeh GI ~25 (Atkinson et al. 2008), tofu GI ~15 (Atkinson et al. 2008), and sambal/salad components with negligible glycemic contribution. Weighted composite GI calculated proportionally to carbohydrate contribution of each component in the 400 g serving. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (48 × 46.0) / 100 ≈ 22.1.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from sambal, marinade, and frying process
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Ayam Penyet to occasional consumption, as a single 400 g serving provides approximately 1680 mg sodium (73% of the 2300 mg daily limit), primarily from sambal paste, spice marinade, and seasoned batter; requesting reduced sambal or sauce can meaningfully lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load and high fat content from deep-frying
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ayam Penyet has a low glycemic index (~48) but delivers a moderate glycemic load (~22 per serving) alongside substantial fat from deep-frying; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size, favour removing the fried batter skin, and pair the dish with non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and sodium from deep-fried chicken and sambal
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The deep-frying process and skin-on chicken thigh contribute approximately 11.2 g saturated fat per serving, and the high sodium load from sambal and marinade elevates cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption, remove the skin and batter, and choose a smaller portion with extra salad.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ayam Penyet is a popular Indonesian dish originating from East Java, widely adopted across Singapore and Southeast Asia. The dish consists of a chicken thigh that is marinated in a fragrant spice paste typically containing galangal, turmeric, lemongrass, garlic, and shallots, then deep-fried until golden and crispy, and finally lightly smashed with a pestle to flatten and tenderise the meat. The smashing technique ('penyet' means 'smashed' in Javanese) creates a characteristic texture that is crispy on the outside and juicy and tender on the inside. Ayam Penyet is traditionally served on a banana leaf or plate alongside fiery sambal terasi (shrimp paste chilli), crispy fried batter pieces, tempeh, tofu, and a fresh side salad of cucumber, cabbage, and tomato. The dish is a staple of Indonesian warungs and has become a mainstream hawker and casual dining item in Singapore. Nutritionally, a standard 400 g serving provides approximately 780 kcal, 56.8 g protein, 43.2 g fat, and 46 g carbohydrate. The dish is high in sodium (~1680 mg per serving) due to sambal and marinade, making it a concern for individuals with hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at 48 (low), though the glycemic load per serving is moderate (~22). Tempeh and tofu contribute plant protein, calcium, and isoflavones. The dish is halal-certified in most Singaporean establishments and is dairy-free but contains soy and gluten allergens from tofu, tempeh, and fried batter respectively.

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for Ayam Penyet as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy fried chicken thigh data and FAO/INFOODS SEA values for tempeh, tofu, and sambal, weighted by approximate component proportions. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for fried chicken and legume-based components. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved databases and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Cuisine tag corrected from 'Coffee' (field survey platform tag) to 'Indonesian', which accurately reflects the dish's origin and culinary category. Food group corrected from 'Grains and staples' to 'Meat and alternatives' as the primary component is chicken. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy or FAO/INFOODS SEA source data for this dish category. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA source data for the composite dish components. 3. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy NDB 05165 and FAO/INFOODS SEA component data. 4. Tamil and Hindi local names are not applicable for this Indonesian dish; these fields are left null as Ayam Penyet is not a Singaporean dish requiring the full Singaporean multilingual matrix. The dish is Indonesian in origin and the mandatory language requirement is Indonesian only (G6). 5. field survey restaurant tags (Coffee, Filipino, Noodle, Cake, Stuffed Pancake, Pancake) are platform categorisation artefacts and are not reflected in the Veritas record. 6. Serving size of 400 g is an estimate based on typical hawker portions observed in Singapore; no standardised serving size exists in approved sources for this dish.
